comments:
Summary and strengths:
This paper addresses an important problem: enabling robots to generalize manipulation trajectories to novel objects by jointly embedding 3D point-clouds (object parts), natural language instructions, and full manipulation trajectories into a common deep feature space. The approach is well-motivated: (1) representing trajectories in task space relative to part principal axes to support direct transfer, (2) learning a deep multimodal embedding with a loss-augmented (loss-based margin) objective to push semantically-irrelevant trajectories away by an amount proportional to their error, (3) proposing a novel pre-training approach tailored to the metric-embedding objective, and (4) handling noisy crowd-sourced demonstrations with a noise-selection/thresholding strategy. The authors introduce Robobarista, a web-based crowdsourcing platform, and publish a sizable dataset (116 objects, 249 parts, 250 instructions, 1225 crowd demonstrations). Evaluation is comprehensive: multiple baselines (including structured SVMs, latent-SSVM, task-similarity methods, deep nets without embedding), a novel DTW-based trajectory distance (DTW-MT) for loss and evaluation, cross-validation experiments, qualitative visualizations of the learned embedding, and real PR2 robot executions demonstrating feasibility. The paper reports that their full model outperforms baselines and that training on crowd data with noise handling beat training on expert-only demos, which is an impactful result for scalability.

Weaknesses and limitations:
- The system depends on segmented object parts selected by human experts; automatic detection/segmentation of manipulable parts (handles/knobs/etc.) is noted as challenging and left out of scope, which limits full autonomy.
- Execution is open-loop (trajectory replay) and does not incorporate visual or force feedback; the authors acknowledge that some tasks (e.g., push until click) will require closed-loop adaptation. This restricts robust real-world applicability for some interactions.
- While the paper compares against a broad set of baselines and ablations, more analysis of sensitivity to hyperparameters (e.g., thresholds tS, tD for noise handling, α for loss augmentation, DTW-MT scaling parameters) and to the amount/quality of crowd data would strengthen claims about robustness.
- Sensor limitations (noisy Kinect point-clouds, glossy/small parts) affect results; experiments show transfers succeed despite noise but higher-quality depth sensing could change performance; discussion could quantify these effects more.

Suggestions for improvement / future work:
- Integrate an automatic part detection/segmentation pipeline (or evaluate robustness when using imperfect automatic segmentation) to move closer to fully autonomous deployment.
- Incorporate closed-loop feedback (vision/force) to adapt transferred trajectories online, and evaluate how much adaptation is required for success on tasks currently failing with open-loop transfer.
- Provide more ablation/sensitivity studies on the noise-handling thresholds, loss-augmentation hyperparameters, and pre-training choices to help reproduce and generalize the approach.
- Release code and dataset (paper indicates dataset/website) and provide practical instructions to reproduce training/inference timings and robot execution steps.

Overall assessment:
This is a solid, well-executed paper that makes multiple meaningful contributions: a principled deep multimodal embedding for transferring manipulation trajectories, a metric and pretraining strategy tailored to the problem, and a large crowd-sourced dataset and platform. The assumptions and limitations are acknowledged and reasonable for a paper of this scope. I recommend acceptance and oral presentation.
